The model

Vintage 2026-07  ·  Built 2026-07-23 20:03 UTC  ·  ← back to the forecast dashboard

The production model in one line: a 32-variable Bayesian VAR in log levels (CBO lineage), estimated on public FRED data from 1986Q1 under an order-invariant stochastic-volatility engine with a fixed Minnesota + sum-of-coefficients prior, publishing five forecast objects each month, with a 12-shock sign-restricted structural layer (RZ-SMC, the Read-Zhu ‘soft-sign’ sequential Monte Carlo sampler) and the previous 28-variable production model running alongside as the shadow. This page is the full description and development record behind that sentence: the pipeline, the model written out symbol by symbol, what goes in and what comes out, every design decision with its evidence, and how the model evolved from the CBO base. It is meant to be readable top to bottom by someone new to the project with a basic time-series background; the interactive BVAR course teaches the underlying concepts in depth, and the forecast dashboard shows the current output.

I · The model, technicallyThe model at a glanceThe model, written outInside stage 2What makes up the modelWhat comes outTwo charts that explain a lot
II · How the model behavesWhat drives the forecastHow the economy responds to shocksThe theory priorAnchor diagnostics
III · Design decisionsEstimation & prior decisionsVariable-set variants
IV · How the model got hereHow the model got hereShadow model cross-checkShadow modelThe compact model
← Forecast dashboard
IThe model, technicallywhat it is, written out, and what it produces

The model at a glance

The pipeline from data to published forecasts. Every decision card below is pinned to one of these six stages — click a stage (here or in the filter row) to see only its decisions.
Two things to carry forward. First, stages 2 and 3 do different jobs that are easy to conflate: the prior (stage 2) restrains the coefficients, while the engine (stage 3) never moves coefficients directly — its stochastic volatility decides how loudly each historical quarter speaks, so wild quarters like 2020 are down-weighted automatically (no Covid dummies needed). Second, the prior and the sum-of-coefficients machinery together produce the signature property of stage 4 — every posterior draw sits just above the unit root (histogram below) — which is correct for trending data and explains several downstream choices. The next two sections make this concrete: first the model written out in symbols, then the prior stage taken apart piece by piece.

The model, written out

The complete production model, in five blocks. Nothing is elided: every distribution below is the one in the estimating code, and the two tables at the end inventory every quantity — the ∼15,300 numbers estimation learns and the handful of dials we set. Amber boxes are the alternatives that were tried, written in the same notation. Each block: the object in words, a picture, then the maths.

(1) One equation generates the data. 1 4

The object first: a vector autoregression is nothing more than 32 regression equations stacked together — one per variable — where the regressors of every equation are the same: everybody's values over the last four quarters. Here is the whole thing as shapes:

Each navy column is the data at one date; each green grid is a 32×32 block of lag weights; the red column is the surprise. Reading one white stripe across gives one ordinary regression: variable i today = weighted sum of everything's recent past + an intercept + a surprise. In symbols:

(1)

where each yt holds the 32 series after two fixed transforms — quantities and price indices enter as (so a difference of 1 ≈ a 1% change) and interest rates as (a convention inherited from the CBO original). With p = 4 lags, the first usable observation is 1987Q1, so estimation sees T = 157 quarters. Two facts to carry forward. Counting unknowns: c and the four B's hold 32 + 4×32×32 = 4,128 lag weights, and the engine below adds ∼11,400 more — against 157×32 ≈ 5,000 data points. Estimation without outside discipline is hopeless; blocks (3) and (4) supply it. And forecasting is just iteration: feed in the last four quarters, draw a surprise, step forward, repeat — the fan chart is that procedure run 1,000 times.

(2) The engine: how loud are the surprises? 3

Before any prior, the model needs to say how the surprises εt behave. The simplest assumption — same loudness always — is visibly wrong in this data:

Quiet mid-2000s, a financial-crisis hump, a Covid spike an order of magnitude larger. The engine models this with three learned parts, and it helps to read the equations bottom-up: (2c) says each variable's loudness drifts slowly; (2e) says a one-off freak quarter can be absorbed by a temporary multiplier instead; (2a) turns the 32 correlated surprises into 32 independent ones so each can have its own loudness.

(2a)
(2b)
(2c)
(2d)
(2e)

Read it as: B0 (2a) is the co-movement pattern — which surprises arrive together. Its prior (2d) centres it on the identity matrix (“start from: surprises don't co-move”) with the same looseness for every element, and that symmetry is the point: it is what makes the whole engine order-invariant — reshuffling the variable list cannot change any result. hi,t (2c) is shock i's loudness on a log scale; its priors say loudness is persistent (ρi near 0.95: this quarter's loudness is mostly last quarter's) and moves in small steps (the InvGamma keeps σ²η small); the hi,1 term just starts the path from its own long-run distribution rather than an arbitrary point. oi,t (2e) is the freak-quarter escape valve: normally exactly 1 (no effect); with small probability pout — held near 1% by the Beta(2,200) prior unless the data insist — it can jump to 2–20 and absorb a spike. 2020Q2 is fitted as a large o, not as a permanent rise in h and not by distorting the coefficients: that one mechanism is the entire Covid treatment. Note what the engine never touches: c and the B's. It only decides how loudly each quarter's evidence speaks when they are estimated.

ALTERNATIVES Homoskedastic (the fallback engine): delete (2b)–(2e) and set Σt = Σ, one constant covariance — simpler, but the fan chart cannot widen in turbulent times and 2020 must be handled by hand. Cholesky-SV (retired): keep everything but force B0 triangular — zeros above the diagonal mean variable 1's surprise may move variable 32's but never the reverse, so the variables are no longer treated symmetrically, and the forecast changed by ∼9% of GDP when the list was merely re-ordered. That asymmetry is exactly what (2d)'s symmetric prior removes.

(3) The coefficient prior: a default and a leash for every lag weight. 2

Discipline number one acts coefficient by coefficient. Every lag weight gets a Normal prior — a default value (its mean) and a leash (its variance, limiting how far data can pull it). The defaults encode “each variable is a random walk that ignores the others”; the leashes are computed by one formula:

(3a)
(3b)

Piece by piece: the indicator in (3a) puts the default at 1 on a variable's own first lag and 0 everywhere else — that IS the random walk. The 0.04 in (3b) is κ, the one tightness dial (a leash of sd 0.2 on first-lag weights). The 1/ℓ² makes leashes shorter on older lags — the model must be very sure before using year-old information. The σ²i/σ²m ratio converts units: σ²j is variable j's typical own-noise, measured once by a small AR(4) pre-regression per variable, so “one leash length” means the same thing whether the regressor is log-GDP or an interest rate. And the intercepts ci are left essentially free (leash 100σ² — a deliberate non-choice with consequences: see the DIO story in block 4). Here are the actual 112 leashes of the GDP equation, computed by this formula from this vintage's data, next to what estimation did with the own-lag defaults:

The heatmap teaches the formula: columns fade left to right (the 1/ℓ² decay), rows differ by the unit conversion (quiet series get short leashes as regressors, noisy ones long). The dot plot shows the outcome: own first-lag weights hug the δ=1 default, drifting only where 157 quarters of evidence argued — the prior holding unless the data object. One disambiguation before moving on, because three “sigma-like” objects are now in play: lower-case = one fixed calibration number per variable (this block only); capital = the estimated, time-varying covariance of the surprises (block 2); and what papers call is nothing but all the Vℓ(i,m) leashes filed into one matrix — implied by (3b), never estimated.

ALTERNATIVE Horseshoe (rejected): replace the fixed 0.04 with learned leashes — , where the global dial κ and a local scale Ψ for every single coefficient get their own priors and are estimated. The intent is to pin most coefficients hard while releasing the few that matter. On trending levels data the learned cross-variable κ collapsed toward zero — the model stopped letting any variable learn from any other — so production rowed back to the fixed leashes above.

(4) The behaviour prior: invented quarters appended to the data. 2

Discipline number two is a different machine entirely. Instead of restraining coefficients one at a time, it teaches the system behaviour — by fabricating a handful of extra data rows and stacking them under the real sample:

The production rows are the sum-of-coefficients (SOC) prior: one invented quarter per variable, in which variable i has sat at — its average over the sample's first four quarters — forever, with the intercept switched off. Written as the row actually fed to equation i:

(4a)

Notice that with the intercept zeroed, the only way the equation fits this row well is if the own-lag weights sum to one — full persistence, “a variable that has been stable stays put”. Nothing forces the fit; the row simply pulls, and dividing through by μ = 0.2 makes the row five times larger, hence — in a least-squares sense — much harder to ignore. Smaller μ would shout louder; μ = 1 would whisper. These 32 rows are why the estimated dynamics sit just above the unit root (the histogram in the charts section), and relaxing them was the root cause of two past failures. The PLR rows (4b, Theory-anchored variant only) are the same construction applied to combinations: pick a theory matrix H whose rows are the term spread, consumption − GDP, and so on, and append one row per combination holding Hiy at its presample average, at volume 1/φ (φ = 0.2). Setting H = identity recovers (4a) exactly — SOC is the special case “every variable is its own combination”. PLR says nothing about where any level should be; only that theory-stable combinations should not trend.

ALTERNATIVES DIO / single-unit-root (rejected) — ONE more invented quarter, for the whole system at once, with the intercept switched on:
(4c)
Compare with (4a): the loaded intercept is the only difference, and it is the whole point — this row can be fit either by unit-root dynamics or by a small intercept, so it disciplines drift that comes from the constant term (which (3a) left essentially free). Tested at every tightness θ from 0.02 to 1.0 on this data: it never binds, the marginal likelihood rejects it by ∼11 log points, and its adaptive variant hurt out-of-sample accuracy — see its card. Villani (mothballed) is not a row at all: it rewrites block (1) around an explicit steady state, , with a prior on itself — elegant where a steady state exists; trending log-levels have none, and it failed exactly that way.

(5) What estimation returns. 4

Bayes' rule multiplies the blocks together:

(5)

The left side is a probability distribution over every unknown at once — the ∼15,300 numbers in the first table below. No formula solves it, so it is sampled by a Gibbs sampler: draw each group of unknowns from its exact conditional distribution given the current values of all the others, and cycle — (i) the lag weights c, B given the engine's state; (ii) B0; (iii) the loudness paths h; (iv) ρ and σ²η; (v) the outlier scales o and pout — 10,000 sweeps, the first 5,000 discarded as warm-up, every 5th kept. What remains is 1,000 stored draws, each a complete, internally-consistent version of the model. Their agreement is what the model knows; their disagreement IS the parameter uncertainty; and everything downstream — fan charts, decompositions, the structural layer — is computed on all 1,000, never on a single “best” estimate.

Everything the sampler learns (the parameters)
ObjectSymbolHow many numbersIts prior
Lag weights & interceptsc, B1…B44,128eq. (3) — the Minnesota default-and-leash prior
Surprise co-movementB01,024each element Normal(1 if i=j else 0, 1) — eq. (2d)
Loudness pathshi,t32 × 157 = 5,024the AR law (2c), started from its own long-run distribution
Loudness persistenceρi32Normal(0.95, 0.04) — eq. (2d)
Loudness step sizeσ²η,i32InvGamma(5, 0.04) — eq. (2d)
Outlier scalesoi,t32 × 157 = 5,024= 1 normally; Uniform{2…20} with prob pout — eq. (2e)
Outlier frequencypout1Beta(2, 200) — prior mean ≈ 1% — eq. (2e)
Total ≈ 15,300 learned numbers, against 157 × 32 ≈ 5,000 data points — the arithmetic fact that makes blocks (3) and (4) non-optional.
Every dial we set (the hyperparameters & design choices)
DialEq.What it controlsWhere decided
δ = 1(3a)the default: every variable a random walkcard: Prior mean
κown = κcross = 0.04(3b)leash length on lag weightscard: Minnesota tightness
κint = 100(3a)intercept leash — deliberately loose (≈ unrestricted)design (CKY convention)
1/ℓ² decay(3b)leashes shorten with squared lag distancedesign rule
σ²j(3b)32 unit-calibration constants, measured by per-variable AR(4) pre-regressions — not chosen, not estimated in the modelmeasured from data
μ = 0.2(4a)volume of the SOC invented quarterscard: Sum-of-coefficients
H, φ = 0.2(4b)the theory combinations and their volume — Theory-anchored variant onlycard: Theory-anchored (PLR)
ρ-prior (0.95, 0.04); InvGamma(5, 0.04); Beta(2, 200); grid {2…20}(2d–e)the engine's own dials — standard literature values (CKY / Stock-Watson), never tuned by usdesign (literature)
p = 4 lags; sample 1986Q1→(1)how much history each equation sees; where the data startcards: Sample start
The rule of thumb. A parameter is anything in the first table: estimation learns it, and it arrives as 1,000 draws. A hyperparameter is anything in the second: a dial we set before estimation that shapes what the model is allowed to believe. Every contested dial has a decision card further down recording why its value was chosen and what else was tried.

Inside stage 2 — the prior, piece by piece

Nearly everything at the prior stage is one of two machines. They attack different problems and do not overlap: channel A restrains individual coefficients; channel B teaches behaviour with invented evidence.
CHANNEL A — a belief about the coefficients
The model has ∼4,100 coefficients and only ∼160 quarters of data. Channel A handles that imbalance: every coefficient gets a default value and a leash. The data can pull a coefficient away from its default, but only as far as the leash allows. (This channel is equation (3) above; its dials are δ and κ.)
Prior mean δ = 1 PRODUCTION
The default itself. Each variable is presumed a random walk — “tomorrow ≈ today” — the right starting belief for trending levels data. Attacks: what should the model believe where the data are silent. (An ADF-adaptive δ that switched defaults per variable was tried and destabilised the long horizon.)
Tightness κ = 0.04 PRODUCTION
The leash length, shared by all coefficients: smaller = tighter hold on the default. Attacks: overfitting. 0.04 is the deliberate compromise — κ=0.08 forecast ∼2% better but made the variance decomposition implausible (each variable explained only by itself).
Lag decay (fixed design) PRODUCTION
Leashes shorten automatically with lag distance (a lag-squared rule), so the model must be very sure before it uses year-old information. Attacks: overfitting where it is worst — the many distant-lag coefficients.
Horseshoe (per-coefficient leashes) REJECTED
Would have let the data choose each leash individually (most coefficients pinned, a few released). Its global dial collapses on trending levels data — cross-variable learning shut down entirely. Rowed back to the fixed leashes above.
CHANNEL B — artificial observations added to the data
Channel B is different machinery: invent a handful of fake data rows that encode an economic belief, and stack them on top of the real sample. The estimation cannot tell them apart from data, so the belief competes with evidence on equal terms — a belief about behaviour, not about any single coefficient. (This channel is block (4) above; the rows are written out there exactly, and μ, φ, θ are their volume dials.)
Sum-of-coefficients μ = 0.2 PRODUCTION
Rows saying “a variable that has been stable stays put” — they discipline each variable's own persistence toward one. Attacks: long-run stability. This is the source of the near-unit-root posterior (the histogram above); relaxing it was the root cause of both the explosive-SV defect and the Villani failure.
PLR, φ = 0.2 (variant prior) PRODUCTION
Rows saying “combinations that theory calls stable should not trend” — the term spread, the consumption-to-GDP ratio — while each individual level stays free. Attacks: theory-free long-run drift in relationships. Run as a separate estimation and published as the Theory-anchored forecast object rather than folded into the baseline.
DIO / single-unit-root REJECTED
One row, built from the sample's opening values, taming the shared deterministic drift. Attacks: the same long-run problem as SOC, via the constant term. Examined three times: it never binds here at any plausible tightness, the evidence rejects it, and its adaptive variant hurt out-of-sample accuracy.
What deliberately is NOT in the prior. Two further disciplines were considered and placed elsewhere. Villani (mothballed) is neither channel — it rebuilds the whole model around an explicit steady state, which trending log-levels do not have. And the long-run anchors (CBO potential, analyst annual figures) could have been prior beliefs but are instead applied after estimation, at stage 5, by reweighting simulated futures: the prior and likelihood stay clean of institutional numbers, and the anchors can change every month without re-estimating the model. When the two channels pull in different directions — channel A holding each variable near a random walk, channel B insisting stable combinations stay stable — the balance is decided by the data through the ordinary Bayesian arithmetic; the dials (κ, μ, φ) set how loudly each side argues.

What comes out — the six forecast objects

Display nameInternalWhat it isModule
Model baselineForecast 1Pure unconditional posterior predictive under oi_svbvar/forecasting.py
Theory-anchoredForecast 5 (PLR)Long-run relationships in the prior (GLP PLR, φ=0.2); differs from the baseline by the prior onlybvar/plr.py
Benchmark-anchoredForecast 2Baseline + joint entropic tilt to CBO potential and FRED/FOMC long-run ratesbvar/longrun_anchors.py
Analyst-anchoredForecast 3 — the analyst pathanalyst-baseline quarterly hard pins (Kalman smoother; growth, inflation, unemployment, fed funds, the 10-year and the analyst's oil-price assumption) + the 5-year annual-average tiltbvar/conditions.py
Analyst-assumptionsForecast 3b — quasi-exogenous variantHard pins on the analyst's technical assumptions only (oil price, fed funds, 10-year); activity and inflation left to the model; long-run tilts unchanged. Reading 3 and 3b together separates the analyst's assumptions from their judgementbvar/conditions.py
Nowcast (current quarter)Forecast 4 (BETA)Monthly mixed-frequency BVAR folded into the baseline's current quarter (--mf-nowcast)bvar/mfbvar.py
How each is produced and read (as published on the dashboard)

The forecast dashboard publishes a regularly updated forecast for the United States from a 32-variable Bayesian vector autoregression (BVAR), estimated on quarterly Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) back to 1986 with four lags.

The model uses order-invariant stochastic volatility (Chan-Koop-Yu 2024) on top of a fixed Minnesota coefficient prior and a sum-of-coefficients prior. The stochastic-volatility specification, which is outlier-robust, lets the width of the uncertainty bands respond to the macroeconomic environment rather than being fixed. The posterior is sampled by Gibbs (10,000 draws, 5,000 burn-in, thinned to 1,000).

The dashboard shows five forecasts that differ only in what is assumed, named by where each one's discipline comes from:

  • Model baseline (grey, headline). The model's own forecast with nothing imposed — the honest view of what the data and the estimated dynamics imply. It fits the short run well; because a levels VAR has no structure forcing convergence, its long horizon drifts (rates and inflation tend to climb), which is exactly what the anchored variants below are there to discipline.
  • Theory-anchored (green). The same model with long-run economic relationships — spreads, expenditure shares, the output gap — built into the prior itself (a "priors for the long run" specification), so the long run is disciplined from inside the model rather than adjusted afterwards. No numeric target is imposed anywhere.
  • Benchmark-anchored (navy). The model's own dynamics, with the long run gently steered toward published reference values — the Congressional Budget Office's potential growth and natural rate of unemployment, the FOMC's longer-run policy rate, and market-based inflation expectations — by entropic tilting at the five-year horizon. Nothing is imposed in the near term; the anchoring is a soft reweighting of the model's own paths, not a hard pin, so the bands stay smooth.
  • Analyst-anchored (red). Conditioned on a central analyst forecast for the near term — growth, inflation, unemployment, both interest rates and the oil-price assumption, imposed as hard quarterly pins through a Kalman filter and a Carter-Kohn smoother for roughly the first two years — and tilted to the analyst's annual averages in the long run.
  • Analyst-assumptions (sienna). A deliberately lighter version of the same idea: only the analyst's assumptions — the oil price, the policy rate and the 10-year yield — are pinned, and the model is left to work out what growth, inflation and unemployment follow from them (long-run tilts unchanged). Reading the two together separates what the analyst assumes from what the analyst concludes.

Shaded areas are nested 66% and 90% credible bands, shown for one forecast at a time (hover or tap a chip above the charts to flip them); following central-bank fan-chart convention they fan out from the last observed data point.

The variance decomposition attributes each variable's forecast uncertainty to groups of structural shocks under a recursive ordering. This is a statistical model output, not advice; long-horizon interest-rate paths are the least reliable element and should be read as indicative.

Two charts that explain a lot

The model is estimated in log levels and every posterior draw's dynamics sit a hair above the stability boundary (median largest root 1.0227). This is the correct behaviour for trending macro data — and the reason the model reports rolling recent-window shock decompositions rather than whole-sample ones, and why long-run information enters through post-estimation tilts rather than in-likelihood priors (see the DIO card below).
The four published forecasts are one posterior wearing four sets of information: nothing (the grey Model baseline, the dashboard's default view), long-run theory (theory-anchored), institutional long-run benchmarks (benchmark-anchored), and the analyst path (analyst-anchored). A fifth object, the current-quarter nowcast, is a separate near-term read rather than a whole-path forecast.
IIHow the model behaveswhat drives the forecast, what drove the economy, how it responds to shocks, and whether the anchors hold

What drives the forecast — variance decomposition

Where the forecast's uncertainty comes from, under two ways of splitting it (tabs): by the variable group the surprise originates in, or by named economic shock. The same decomposition under the shadow model (the previous production model, run alongside each vintage) is in the Shadow model section below. This is a property of the estimated model's own dynamics — the Model baseline forecast; the anchors reweight paths afterwards and do not change it. Volatility state: the group tab is evaluated at the posterior-mean (regime-neutral) volatility state; the named tab's rotations act on the end-of-sample (terminal) covariance, so under stochastic volatility the two tabs answer the question at slightly different volatility states.

By variable group — recursive ordering

Share of each variable's forecast-error variance attributable to each shock group, by horizon. Shocks are grouped by the variable they originate in, under the model's recursive (Cholesky) ordering. Hover for shares.
How to read this. Each bar splits the uncertainty in a variable's forecast into the share coming from each family of shocks. A tall navy segment for GDP, for example, means most of the uncertainty about future GDP comes from real-activity shocks rather than from prices or financial conditions. Near term, a variable's own shocks dominate; further out, other blocks contribute more as the dynamics propagate. The split uses a recursive (Cholesky) ordering, so the exact shares depend on that ordering — but the broad ranking (which shocks matter most) is generally robust.

By named shock — sign restrictions

The same uncertainty split, but by named economic shock: twelve shocks identified by sign and ranking restrictions (Demand, Investment, Financial, Monetary, Gov Spending, Oil Supply, Housing/Credit, Uncertainty, Technology, Labour Supply, Wage Bargaining, Tax), pooled here into blocks (including the combined Financial+Uncertainty family where both are named; the split within that family is softer than the family total, which is the robust object), plus an exact Unidentified remainder. Mean shares across 250 accepted identifications. Identified by a soft-sign importance sampler, with every admissible posterior draw carrying equal weight (a declared modelling choice; Uhlig 2017). Hover for shares.
How this fits. The variable-group tab asks “which variable’s surprises matter”; this tab asks “which economic story do they belong to” — the demand-type family, the supply-type family, or monetary policy. Reading the grey band. With this identification method, naming 12 of the model’s 32 shocks implies a ∼62% expected “Unidentified” share before any data are consulted (as a rule of thumb for set identification, not a defect); the measured grey for GDP at 5 years is 60%, to be read against that ∼62% baseline rather than against zero. The block sums shown here are the robust objects — they do not depend on how the method rotates shocks within a block. And the grey pool is not mysterious: a single statistically-extracted “main business-cycle” shock accounts for a median 89% of unemployment’s business-cycle variance on its own, and 55% even after removing everything the twelve named shocks explain — the unnamed variance is concentrated, not diffuse, but naming its economic cause would require assumptions this dashboard does not impose (see docs/structural_tools.md). Prior vs posterior. At the same posterior parameters, unrestricted random rotations alone give a named-total share for GDP at 2 years of 37% [27–46] before the restrictions are imposed; the restricted posterior delivers 42% [31–52]. The gap is what the identifying restrictions contribute — a modelling choice the data never test (Baumeister-Hamilton 2015); the reduced-form covariance both share is what the data determine. Of the posterior band's variance, ~65% is parameter uncertainty across draws and ~35% is rotation (identification) uncertainty within draws.

How the economy responds to shocks — impulse responses

Five structural views of the same model, one shown at a time (tabs): the named shocks of the production identification (the menu behind the variance and decomposition sections), a recursive (timing) identification of the financial shocks, sign restrictions for demand and monetary, external instruments for the monetary shock, and the max-share business-cycle shock (found by search, not assumption). That the methods agree where each is valid is a check on the structural machinery; each tab carries its own commentary and caveats.

Named shocks — the production scheme's full menu

All 12 named shocks of the production identification (the same accepted identifications behind the variance and decomposition sections, equally weighted where the soft-sign sampler supplied them), each a one-standard-deviation structural shock traced across the five headline responses over 12 quarters. Shaded area is the 66% band, pooling identification and parameter uncertainty. The solid navy line is the pointwise median — a set summary that is not any single admissible model's response; the thin dashed sienna line is the closest-to-median rotation, one coherent model chosen for being nearest the median across every panel at once (Fry-Pagan 2011). Where the two diverge, the median is splicing different models. Hover for each shock's direction convention.
How this fits. The other tabs show selected shocks under alternative identification methods; this one shows the production menu itself — the same shocks whose variance shares and historical contributions the sections above report, so the three displays describe one identification. Each column is shown in its identifying direction (hover): most are expansionary normalisations, Monetary is a tightening, Oil Supply is an adverse supply disruption, and Uncertainty is displayed as a rise in uncertainty (it is identified through its calm mirror image; under linearity the flip is exact). Magnitudes are one-SD shocks rather than headline units, so compare shapes and relative sizes across shocks rather than reading absolute policy magnitudes; bands are wide by construction — sign restrictions set-identify, and the spread across accepted rotations is part of the honest uncertainty.

Recursive identification (CEGSQ) — financial shocks

Each financial shock (a row) traced across the five headline responses (columns), over 12 quarters. Each column has its own y-axis — % deviation for output/prices, percentage points for rates. Hover for values.
Shock sizes (illustrative episode magnitudes): Risk-off (credit spread +0.5pp) · Uncertainty (policy uncertainty +50%) · Equity sell-off (equities -10%). Because the model is linear, the responses scale in proportion to the shock.

Which shocks, and why these. These are the shocks this model identifies reliably — financial / risk shocks. Identification is by conditional forecasting (CEGSQ): the shocked financial variable is moved while the slow-moving real variables are held at their no-shock path for the first quarter (a recursive contemporaneous restriction), and the response is the difference from the no-shock forecast. Because financial variables are “fast” (they can react within the quarter), this ordering identifies them cleanly. Not shown: a monetary-policy shock (on the post-1986 sample the rate–activity comovement is demand-dominated, so a recursive funds-rate shock comes out wrong-signed) and an oil-supply shock (it requires sign restrictions plus an oil-production series this model does not carry). See the methodology note. One caveat shared by all the responses: the model is in levels, so a shock’s effect on a level (output, prices) is persistent rather than fading to zero.

Sign-restricted identification — demand & monetary

An aggregate demand shock and a monetary shock (rows), each traced across the same five responses (columns), identified by sign restrictions (a different method than the recursive tab), over 12 quarters. Demand is normalised to GDP +1% at impact, monetary to a +50bp funds-rate rise. Shaded area is the 66% band. Hover for values.
How this fits. The recursive tab identifies shocks by a timing assumption. Sign restrictions are a different scheme: keep every model whose responses match an assumed sign pattern (a demand shock raises output and prices, etc.). That the two methods agree on the demand shock (output, prices and employment all move the textbook way) is a good check that the structural machinery works. The monetary shock is the interesting case: the recursive panel can’t identify it on this sample (the rate–activity comovement is demand-dominated, so a recursive funds-rate shock comes out wrong-signed), whereas sign restrictions, by imposing the uncontroversial contractionary pattern (a rate rise that cools output and prices), recover the textbook transmission. The honest cost: it is set-identified, so the bands are wider and the pattern is partly assumed rather than discovered (only a minority of admissible models satisfy it — acceptance Demand 99%, Monetary 49%).

External instruments — Bauer-Swanson monetary surprises

A monetary shock identified from outside the model: minute-level interest-rate moves around every Fed announcement (Bauer-Swanson, 1988–2023), summed by quarter, pick out the part of the model’s surprises that is pure policy news. Rows: the raw surprise series and the information-purged variant (the Fed’s predictable response to news stripped out); both normalised to a +25bp funds-rate rise. Shaded area is the 66% band. Hover for values.
How this fits. The sign-restrictions tab assumes the contractionary pattern; an external instrument lets the data supply it. The result is a cautionary tale worth seeing: the raw surprises are a strong instrument but come out wrong-signed (activity rises after a “tightening”) — at quarterly frequency they inherit the Fed’s tendency to surprise hawkish when the economy is strong, and that direction is statistically supported and robust (Raw surprises: robust F 21, GDP direction t +2.2). The info-purged series points the textbook way but is too faint at quarterly frequency to be conclusive (Info-purged: robust F 3, GDP direction t -0.7, not significant; shown to 12 quarters; its point pattern also depends on how the 2020 observations — which the data provider invalidates — are treated). Bands reflect model uncertainty only, not instrument-sampling uncertainty. Full diagnostics: the monetary-proxy experiment’s Anderson-Rubin tests.

The main business-cycle shock — max-share

The single shock that explains the most unemployment variance at business-cycle horizons (1½–8 years) — found by direct search over every possible shock, one per posterior draw, no sign assumptions. It accounts for a median 89% of that variance on its own. Traced across the same five responses over 12 quarters; sized at one standard deviation, signed so unemployment rises. Shaded area is the 66% band, spanning parameter and identification uncertainty jointly. Hover for values.
How this fits. The variance tab shows that no small set of named shocks can claim much of the model's variance (an arithmetic property of that method, not a defect). This tab asks the reverse question: is the business cycle one big force or many small ones? The answer is emphatically one — a single statistically-extracted shock carries most of unemployment's cyclical variance, and its responses look like a recession in the quantities: activity falls, unemployment rises, the policy rate is cut. Prices, notably, barely move — itself a clue that this force is not a pure textbook demand shock. The honest caveat is the label. This method finds the shape of the dominant business-cycle force but cannot name its economic cause — “the thing that moves unemployment” may blend demand, financial and other impulses. Naming it would require assumptions (sign restrictions, instruments) of the kind the other tabs impose. The two approaches are complementary: named shocks with modest coverage, or dominant coverage without a name.

The theory prior — mechanics, and the evidence it works

What it is. The theory-anchored forecast adds a prior on long-run relationships (Giannone, Lenza & Primiceri 2019) to an otherwise identical model: a set of artificial pre-observations telling the estimator that certain economically meaningful combinations of the variables — interest-rate spreads, expenditure shares, the output gap — should not drift apart over long horizons. No individual series is pinned to any value; only the combinations are disciplined, and gently. What this project did to make it work at 32 variables. The prior requires naming one long-run combination per variable, and no published application exists at anything near this scale (the original paper works with seven variables; the New York Fed’s 33-variable model considered the prior and left it for future work, citing the difficulty). Our construction is a hybrid: 20 combinations we have genuine theory for (the spreads and great ratios charted below) and 12 default rows that reduce exactly to the standard sum-of-coefficients treatment — supplying the additional a-priori structure the paper itself says large systems need. How tight, and why that was a judgement. The prior’s overall tightness was fixed by hand (φ = 0.2) rather than estimated: the data-preferred setting produces decade-ahead GDP drift of 4.15 %/yr, far above any published potential-growth estimate, while φ = 0.2 lands the model at 1.92 %/yr — the CBO’s neighbourhood. Fixing tightness by judgement is one of the source paper’s three sanctioned options, and the paper’s authors themselves override their estimated tightness — fully dogmatically — when it produced pathological long-horizon forecasts; our choice is a milder version of the same move, calibrated so an observable (long-run drift) is plausible. Guards. The known failure modes are the paper’s own and are monitored: the row-scale pathology its footnote 4 warns about is logged per estimation; genuinely trending ratios are left free (see the wage-bill and consumption charts below); and a plausibility bound discards the small fraction of draws (~6%) with extreme implied paths. Each chart below states what the combination is, what a good forecast should do with it, and what it demonstrates the prior delivering. Tested, and not promoted. In July 2026 the two priors were raced in real time on 77 forecast origins under a decision rule fixed in advance (the full record is on the forecast page’s Track record section, and the archived decision memo in the repository). The theory prior kept long-horizon uncertainty bands dramatically better behaved and slightly improved real-activity forecasts, but its one-year CPI forecasts were significantly worse (about 12%) — robust across every pre-planned check — so the plain model remains the default and this prior remains the published long-run-discipline alternative. The likely mechanism (under investigation) is the scale-sensitivity of the nominal ratio rows on real-time data, the same fragility the source paper’s footnote 4 warns about.
HistoryModel baseline (median)Theory-anchored (median, nested 66/90% bands shown)
term spread (10Y − FF) — stationary in-sample
The 10-year yield minus the fed funds rate. Theory (the expectations hypothesis plus a bounded term premium) says the two rates can each go anywhere, but the gap between them should keep fluctuating around a stable level. What to look for: both forecasts should mean-revert; the theory prior should hold the spread in a stable corridor rather than letting the two rates drift apart. What it shows the prior doing: disciplining the rate block jointly — no single rate is pinned to any number.
output gap (GDP − Potential) — stationary in-sample
Real GDP relative to CBO potential — stationary almost by definition of potential. What to look for: the flagship demonstration. The plain model lets the gap drift over the forecast; the theory prior holds it fluctuating near zero. What it shows the prior doing: exactly the discipline it was adopted for — the long-run path stays economically coherent without any hard-coded number for GDP itself.
wage bill share (wages − GDP) — trends in-sample — a trending forecast is correct
The wage bill relative to nominal output. In the post-1986 data this ratio trends, so a trending forecast is the model being right, not drifting. What to look for: both forecasts continuing the gentle historical trend. What it shows the prior doing: staying out of the way — the prior on dimensionless combinations is deliberately mild, so it does not fight ratios the data say are genuinely trending. A prior that flattened this line would be imposing false theory.
consumption share (C − Y) — trends in-sample — a trending forecast is correct
Consumption relative to output — the classic 'great ratio', and another one that trends in the sample. What to look for: the same as the wage bill share: a continued gentle trend from both forecasts. What it shows the prior doing: the source paper's own evidence says the prior tracks rather than fights this ratio, and the chart is our replication of that behaviour on US data.
labour share (compensation − productivity) — slow reversion in-sample
Real hourly compensation relative to labour productivity — whether workers' pay tracks what they produce. Slow reversion in-sample. What to look for: a slow pull back toward the historical relationship, with honest (wide) bands — not a hard snap. What it shows the prior doing: encoding a weak, patient version of a classic long-run relationship.
receipts share (receipts − GDP) — stationary in-sample
Real federal receipts relative to GDP — the effective average tax take, mean-reverting through policy over the sample. What to look for: reversion toward the historical range at an unhurried pace. What it shows the prior doing: keeping a fiscal ratio anchored so long-horizon paths do not imply an implausible tax share.

Anchor diagnostics

The forecasts above are disciplined by external anchors, imposed by reweighting the model's posterior draws until each anchor is met. This table reports the health of that step for the current vintage: how many constraints were met exactly, and the minimum effective sample size (ESS) — the share of draws still carrying weight after reweighting. Low ESS means an anchor sits far from the model's own view; below 0.30 the anchor is refused rather than published on thin evidence.
Anchor set Applies toConstraints met Min ESSStatus
CBO long-run anchorsBenchmark-anchored forecast5/50.72ok
Analyst long-run anchorsAnalyst-anchored forecast5/50.57ok
Analyst long-run anchorsAnalyst-anchored forecast5/50.50ok

VariableConstraintTargetAchievedESS

CPI-UYear-on-year %2.43%2.43%0.72
Unemployment RateAnnual average4.16%4.16%0.72
Fed Funds RateAnnual average3.10%3.10%0.72
TR10y RateAnnual average4.46%4.46%0.72

GDPAnnual average27,097 $bn27,097 $bn0.57
CPI-UAnnual average374.4374.40.57
Unemployment RateAnnual average4.30%4.30%0.57

IIIDesign decisionsevery estimation and prior choice, and the variable-set variants

Estimation & prior decisions

Each card: what production uses, what was considered, why, and the evidence pack. Badges: green = in production / defended, red = examined and rejected or rowed back, grey = retired/mothballed. The outlined chip is the pipeline stage the decision belongs to.

Volatility engine

PRODUCTION 3 · ENGINE
Chosen: Order-invariant stochastic volatility (Chan-Koop-Yu 2024) with an outlier-robust layer ('oi_sv')
Considered / rejected: Homoskedastic (kept as fallback); Cholesky-SV ('full_sv', retired from the CLI — ordering-dependent, ~9% GDP sensitivity to variable order, and carries the Bognanni-2022 equation-by-equation draw bias)
Why: Best GDP forecast-accuracy score at every horizon (CRPS — the Continuous Ranked Probability Score, which grades the whole predictive distribution, lower better); order-invariant; Covid-robust without dummies; SV carried through the conditional and nowcast layers.
Evidence: docs/engine_modes.md; docs/model_testing_and_decisions.md §2.1

Coefficient prior under SV

ROWED BACK 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: Fixed Minnesota (κ_own = κ_cross = 0.04, the textbook Litterman design)
Considered / rejected: Chan-Koop-Yu's horseshoe global-local prior (collapsed on levels data: cross-shrinkage → 0, the variance decomposition (FEVD) degenerated to 100% own-shock) and the own-lag-horseshoe variant (same collapse, opposite direction)
Why: I(1) levels data breaks the horseshoe's global scale (Piironen-Vehtari 2017 mechanism); fixed Minnesota keeps the CRPS advantage with a balanced FEVD.
Evidence: docs/oisv_cross_prior_decision.md

Minnesota tightness κ = 0.04

PRODUCTION 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: κ = 0.04, confirmed set-optimal on the 28-variable set and re-confirmed on the 32-variable production set (doc 09 §2)
Considered / rejected: κ = 0.08 (forecasts ~2% better but collapses the FEVD own-share to an implausible level)
Why: A forecast-defensible compromise that keeps the structural layer interpretable; flagged as ideally size-adaptive.
Evidence: docs/calibration/03, 07

Sum-of-coefficients prior μ = 0.2

PRODUCTION 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: SOC at 0.2 (inherited from the R original, interrogated and retained July 2026)
Considered / rejected: Removing or loosening it
Why: Underpins the near-unit-root stability; its removal was the root cause of the explosive-SV defect and the Villani failure.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/15; NOTES.md 2026-07-02

Single-unit-root (DIO) prior

REJECTED 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: Kept OUT of production (default-off research flag)
Considered / rejected: Sims (1993) dummy-initial-observation prior at any tightness θ ∈ [0.02, 1.0]
Why: Three separate examinations: no stabilising effect on the companion roots at any tightness (median root ~1.008 throughout); the marginal likelihood rejects a binding DIO by ~11 log points; the Giannone-Lenza-Primiceri size-adaptive variant over-shrinks long-horizon real activity out of sample. Long-run information lives in the post-hoc tilting layer instead.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/01_prior_ablation.md; calibration/02; econometrics/14

Prior mean: random walk (δ=1)

PRODUCTION 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: Random-walk-centred Minnesota prior mean
Considered / rejected: ADF-adaptive prior mean ('rejected, catastrophically')
Why: The levels system wants the unit-root centring; the adaptive variant destabilised the long horizon.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/13, 14

Covid handling

PRODUCTION 3 · ENGINE
Chosen: Outlier-robust SV absorbs 2020 (production); Lenza-Primiceri observation downweighting (covid_scale) available on the structural/homoskedastic path
Considered / rejected: Covid dummy variables (the Bank of England's route)
Why: The outlier layer handles the tail without changing the deterministic component; structural decompositions must footnote that 2020 re-identifies as huge shocks.
Evidence: docs/engine_modes.md; NOTES.md

Sample start 1986Q1

PRODUCTION 1 · DATA
Chosen: 1986Q1 (forced by two late-starting variables: EPU, Cleveland Fed inflation expectations)
Considered / rejected: 1974/1976 starts (used in structural experiments)
Why: The cost is documented: excluding the Volcker disinflation is why recursive monetary identification attenuates on the production sample; the 1976+/market-rate variant recovers the textbook contraction (--volcker).
Evidence: docs/calibration/06; docs/econometrics/03

Long-run anchoring: tilt-only

PRODUCTION 5 · FORECAST LAYER
Chosen: Entropic annual-average tilts for every variable (joint solver)
Considered / rejected: Hard anchor-pins at the 5-year quarter for rates (rowed back: produced the visible 'diabolo' — band collapsing to a point then re-widening)
Why: The pin was a stabiliser for the old mildly explosive homoskedastic engine; under oi_sv it is unnecessary and the tilt matches the annual-average data semantics.
Evidence: CLAUDE.md D6; docs/three_forecast_layers.md

Tilting solver

PRODUCTION 5 · FORECAST LAYER
Chosen: Joint entropic solver across the ~5 correlated long-run constraints
Considered / rejected: Sequential composition (strained ESS when constraints interact)
Why: Healthy effective sample sizes with all constraints met simultaneously.
Evidence: docs/three_forecast_layers.md

Villani steady-state BVAR

MOTHBALLED 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: Levels + I(1) dynamics + post-hoc tilts
Considered / rejected: Villani (2009) steady-state prior parameterisation
Why: Requires a stationary demeaned VAR; anchoring an I(1) log-level via a deterministic trend is out of domain — the unconditional forecast exploded once SOC was relaxed.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/villani_postmortem.md; mothballed/villani/

Theory-anchored forecast (PLR φ=0.2)

PRODUCTION 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: The Giannone-Lenza-Primiceri (2019) 'priors for the long run' at SOC-equivalent tightness φ=0.2 — the 5th forecast object
Considered / rejected: φ=1 (median companion root 1.057, too loud)
Why: A clean one-factor comparison against the baseline: same model, long-run theory added in the prior.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/10, 15-18; docs/design/plr_forecast_object.md

Constant parameters

DEFENDED 3 · ENGINE
Chosen: Constant-parameter BVAR (TVP not adopted)
Considered / rejected: Time-varying-parameter variants
Why: The SV layer captures the volatility instability that motivates most TVP use; parameter drift not supported at this sample size.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/11_constant_params_defence.md

Shadow-model shock menu (nine named shocks)

SHADOW MENU 6 · STRUCTURAL LAYER
Chosen: Nine named economic shocks for the 28-variable shadow model: seven core shocks (demand, investment, financial, monetary, government spending, a generic supply shock, and oil supply) plus a housing/credit shock and a policy-uncertainty shock. They are told apart by the signs their responses must take, plus a few ranking rules that say which shock moves a given variable more (15 such rules, at most three per shock). The financial and uncertainty shocks look alike, so they are separated by a rule matched to the relative size of the policy-uncertainty and credit-spread responses, and are always also reported as a combined financial-plus-uncertainty share in case the split is imperfect.
Considered / rejected: Richer schemes that proved infeasible: a full battery ranking every demand-type shock against every separator (each rule is plausible on its own, but jointly they are almost never satisfied, about four accepted draws per billion); rules imposed over several horizons at once (which crushed the acceptance rate); an agnostic monetary shock that leaves the output response unrestricted; and a foreign-activity shock, dropped for want of a clean real-time series.
Why: The scheme follows the minimal-separation principle of Chan, Matthes & Yu (2025): use the fewest distinguishing rules that still tell each pair of shocks apart, rather than an exhaustive ranking battery, so the identification stays feasible. Since July 2026 this menu identifies the shadow model; the production model uses the larger 12-shock menu in the next card.
Evidence: docs/research/tier_design_2026-07.md; shock_menu_2026-07.md

32-variable production model + 12-shock menu (2026-07)

PRODUCTION 6 · STRUCTURAL LAYER
Chosen: In July 2026 the production model grew from 28 to 32 variables, adding labour productivity, real hourly compensation, real federal receipts and house prices. The aim was sharper structural identification, not better forecasts: these four series let the model break the single generic supply shock into three distinct ones (technology, labour supply and wage bargaining, told apart by the signs of the real wage and unemployment) and add a tax shock disciplined by the receipts series (the Mountford-Uhlig fiscal-identification trick). That gives a 12-shock menu the 28-variable set cannot separate. It was promoted only after clearing the same bar the previous model did: it passed the release checks with zero failures (87 checks, 7 non-blocking warnings), reconfirmed the incumbent's tuning, and matched it across all four parts of the real-time accuracy backtest set out in advance, so it forecasts no worse than the 28-variable model.
Considered / rejected: Brute-force rejection sampling for the 12-shock menu: it admits the scheme only about four times per five million random rotations, far too rare to use, so the model instead uses the Read-Zhu ‘soft-sign’ importance sampler (RZ-SMC), which reaches the same posterior draw by draw (every one of the 1,000 production draws admits the scheme). Also rejected as extra variables: unit labour costs (already implied by compensation, productivity and prices), a bank-lending-standards series (data start only in 1990), and a foreign-activity series (no clean real-time vintages).
Why: The gain is identification, not forecasting: on the production posterior the named shocks jointly explain 42% of GDP variance at a two-year horizon, up from roughly 26–39% before, thanks to the supply split and the disciplined tax shock. Because the expanded model forecasts to the same accuracy as the 28-variable one, the latter keeps running alongside as a shadow cross-check.
Evidence: docs/research/tierb32_candidate_2026-07.md; tier_design_2026-07.md

Structural identification set

PRODUCTION 6 · STRUCTURAL LAYER
Chosen: Four identification methods, each used only where it is trustworthy: a recursive (timing-based) identification for the financial shocks; sign-restricted structural VARs, including the named-shock menus above; the Baumeister-Hamilton informative-prior method for the oil shock; and external instruments for the monetary shock. On top sits the named-shock decomposition layer (the Bank of England presentation idiom) that attributes past movements to those shocks.
Considered / rejected: Using a raw external instrument as the production oil identification: it is contaminated by demand at quarterly frequency (retired April 2026, reconfirmed July 2026). And a nine-named-shock extension whose extra ranking rules collapse the acceptance rate (a recorded negative result).
Why: Each method is used only where it is well identified; the decision guide is documented separately.
Evidence: docs/structural_tools.md

Estimand of the set-identified objects

PRODUCTION 6 · STRUCTURAL LAYER
Chosen: Conditionally-uniform over the admissible set (the RZ soft-sign engine: every retained posterior draw carries equal weight, with rotations uniform within each draw's non-empty admissible set) for the production 12-shock objects; every output (variance shares, decompositions, shock series, impulse responses) is labelled with the weighting it uses.
Considered / rejected: Presenting set summaries as if they were a single model's objects (the Fry-Pagan pitfall): pointwise medians across rotations splice different admissible models, so the dashboard now overlays a closest-to-median single rotation on the named IRFs and labels every pooled mean as a set summary. Also rejected: silently mixing this estimand with the shadow model's joint-uniform accept-reject numbers (the two are labelled wherever they appear together; measured ≤1.2pp apart on headline shares).
Why: Set identification means the data plus restrictions deliver a set of admissible models, not one; which weighting over that set is reported is a modelling choice that must be stated, not implied. The identification prior over rotations is never updated by the data within the set (Baumeister-Hamilton 2015), so the prior-vs-posterior exhibit on the variance page shows what the restrictions contribute versus what the data determine.
Evidence: docs/research/read_zhu_port_2026-07.md §estimand; bvar/sign_restrictions.py (design notes); docs/research/fevd_large_vars_2026-07.md

Variable-set variants

SetStatusNotes
tierb32 (32 vars)PRODUCTION since 2026-07big_financial + labor productivity, real hourly compensation, real federal receipts, FHFA house prices; promoted on the identification gain from the 12-shock menu (docs/calibration/09), and forecast-neutral against the previous model
big_financial (28 vars)SHADOW since 2026-07 (--candidate big_financial)the 24-var CBO set + Baa-10Y spread, housing starts, NASDAQ, trade-weighted USD; promoted on identification (correct risk-off sign), forecast-equivalent
original (24 vars)rollback (--candidate original)the CBO/Yoo set; prior production
combined / financial / oil_supply (25-28)tested, not adoptedcalibration-program candidates; the early 'more vars = worse' result was substantially a prior artifact
23var-oil / 25var (1974+)structural experiments onlylonger-sample oil-identification sets (Blanchard attenuation, SBVAR)
The calibration arc in one paragraph: a naive post-Covid screen said “add oil and financial variables”; the honest real-time re-run, with the forecast-accuracy differences tested for statistical significance, said “keep the incumbent”; a further pass showed that negative result was substantially a prior artifact; and big_financial was then promoted on identification grounds (the credit spread carries the correct risk-off sign) while remaining forecast-equivalent. Evidence: docs/calibration/00–08.
IVHow the model got herethe development arc, and the shadow model that runs alongside as a cross-check

How the model got here

The development arc from the CBO base to the current production model — the big changes only, each argued in the evidence packs linked from the cards above.
2023-08
The methodology base
CBO Working Paper 2023-08 (Yoo): a 24-variable Bayesian VAR in log levels with Minnesota + sum-of-coefficients priors — the design this model descends from.
2026-01
R implementation
The 24-variable R model (us_bvar): Kalman-smoother conditional forecasting on an analyst baseline, mixed-frequency handling, scenario machinery.
2026-02
Python port
Full reimplementation on free FRED data with an R-match test suite; analyst baselines move from Excel to an API feed with local caching.
2026-03
The tilting layer
Entropic tilting added as the long-run anchoring mechanism — reweighting simulated paths after estimation rather than putting long-run dogma in the prior. First external econometric review.
2026-04
Structural toolkit, round 1
Sign-restricted identification (SBVAR) added; the raw-instrument proxy SVAR tried and retired (demand-contaminated at quarterly frequency); peer review and cleanup.
2026-05
Production hardening
Economic-validation release gates, golden-master tests, the production dashboard, and a full-model review.
2026-06
The engine month
Order-invariant stochastic volatility adopted as the production engine; its horseshoe coefficient prior collapsed on levels data and was rowed back to a fixed Minnesota (κ=0.04). The 28-variable big_financial set promoted. Hard rate pins replaced by tilt-only anchoring, removing the band artefact where the fan collapsed to a point at the anchor quarter then re-widened.
2026-07
Five forecast objects; structural suite, round 2
The PLR Theory-anchored forecast ships as the fifth object; the monthly mixed-frequency nowcast folds into the current quarter; joint tilting replaces sequential. External-instrument monetary identification, a twelve-shock structural menu (nine on the shadow model) with ranking restrictions, the Bank-of-England-style decomposition layer, and the variance-decomposition coverage toolkit (block shares + max-share) join the standing toolkit.

Shadow model cross-check

The Model baseline forecast (Forecast 1, the model's own unconditional view) from the production model (tierb32, 32 variables) next to the same object from the shadow model (big_financial, 28 variables — the production model until July 2026, re-run on this vintage's data). Calendar-year averages; growth rates are year-over-year of the annual average, in %. The two forecast alike, checked across a range of accuracy tests when the current model was adopted; this table is the standing monthly check that they stay that way.
202620272028
32v28v32v28v32v28v
Real GDP growth2.32.32.52.72.52.6
CPI inflation3.13.33.43.93.13.8
PCE inflation3.53.63.33.73.03.5
Unemployment rate4.44.34.44.24.44.1
Fed funds rate3.43.53.33.73.34.0
10-year Treasury4.24.24.04.34.04.3

Shadow model — variance & historical decomposition

The shadow model's own structural decompositions under its nine-shock menu, the counterparts to the production model's 12-shock versions in Part II. Agreement across the two is the cross-model robustness check; divergence marks where the current model's four extra variables change the reading. Hover for values.
Variance decomposition · 9-shock menu

By named shock — sign restrictions

The same uncertainty split, but by named economic shock: nine shocks identified by sign and ranking restrictions (Demand, Investment, Financial, Monetary, Gov Spending, Supply, Oil Supply, Housing/Credit, Uncertainty), pooled here into blocks (including the combined Financial+Uncertainty family where both are named; the split within that family is softer than the family total, which is the robust object), plus an exact Unidentified remainder. Mean shares across 250 accepted identifications. Hover for shares.
How this fits. The variable-group tab asks “which variable’s surprises matter”; this tab asks “which economic story do they belong to” — the demand-type family, the supply-type family, or monetary policy. Reading the grey band. With this identification method, naming 9 of the model’s 28 shocks implies a ∼68% expected “Unidentified” share before any data are consulted (as a rule of thumb for set identification, not a defect); the measured grey for GDP at 5 years is 67%, to be read against that ∼68% baseline rather than against zero. The block sums shown here are the robust objects — they do not depend on how the method rotates shocks within a block. And the grey pool is not mysterious: a single statistically-extracted “main business-cycle” shock accounts for a median 91% of unemployment’s business-cycle variance on its own, and 58% even after removing everything the nine named shocks explain — the unnamed variance is concentrated, not diffuse, but naming its economic cause would require assumptions this dashboard does not impose (see docs/structural_tools.md). Prior vs posterior. At the same posterior parameters, unrestricted random rotations alone give a named-total share for GDP at 2 years of 37% [27–46] before the restrictions are imposed; the restricted posterior delivers 42% [31–52]. The gap is what the identifying restrictions contribute — a modelling choice the data never test (Baumeister-Hamilton 2015); the reduced-form covariance both share is what the data determine. Of the posterior band's variance, ~65% is parameter uncertainty across draws and ~35% is rotation (identification) uncertainty within draws.
Historical decomposition · 9-shock menu
checks pass
How to read this chart
How to read one date. Take any quarter of the GDP panel: the gap between the dashed and solid lines is what shocks did to y/y growth over the preceding five years, and the bars name the families responsible — blue above the dashed line means demand-type shocks were lifting growth, orange below means supply-type shocks were dragging it (purple = policy & financial, gold = fiscal). GDP and CPI are shown as year-on-year rates (the four-quarter change, decomposed with both endpoints measured against the same five-year-earlier anchor, so the pieces add up exactly); the unemployment rate and the 10-year Treasury yield are decomposed in levels. The default view is the last three years; the second chip shows the full history back to 2007. Five honest caveats. The baseline is re-anchored each quarter (always five years back), so read the bars vertically at a date rather than comparing bar-to-bar across long spans; shock recovery uses each posterior draw's end-of-sample volatility state. The bars are means across accepted identifications (a set of admissible structural models, equally weighted) — a set summary that adds up exactly but is not any single admissible model's decomposition; the family blocks are the robust object, single columns the softer one. The near-black “Covid 2020” bar carries the transitory pandemic dislocation: the component of the 2020Q1–Q3 shocks that the model’s outlier layer (Carriero-Clark-Marcellino-Mertens 2024) classifies as one-off, plus its knock-on effect through the window anchor. The named bars carry only the persistent part of those quarters’ shocks, so they stay interpretable through the pandemic (the Bank of England’s convention for its own decomposition charts). The bar is exactly zero from 2025Q4, when the 20-quarter window clears 2020. The grey “Unidentified” pool is expected to be large (naming 9 of 28 shocks implies a ∼68% unnamed share by construction — see the variance decomposition on the model page); at the zero-lower-bound episodes it also absorbs genuine demand shocks, whose required interest-rate co-movement the ZLB suppresses. And the giant 2020 bars are real, not an artefact: they are the pandemic hitting inside the window.

The compact model — specification & estimation

The interpretation-only compact structural model shown on the forecast page, in full. Variables (11, quarterly, FRED, 1985Q1–): the Kilian global-activity index (IGREA; entering as published — levels, no re-detrending, own-lag prior mean 0.9, exempt from the unit-root dummy priors), the real WTI price, CPI energy, real GDP, the unemployment rate, labour productivity and real compensation (from the BLS Productivity and Costs release), headline CPI, the federal funds rate, the Baa–10Y spread, and the policy-uncertainty index. Identification: impact-only exact zeros, sign restrictions and magnitude cross-rankings, the Arias, Rubio-Ramírez and Waggoner (2018) Algorithm 2 construction with the Algorithm-3 volume-weight correction (with a stated weighting over the admissible identifications, applied consistently to every output). (r) marks the magnitude rankings: Financial moves the spread by more (in magnitude) than US demand; Uncertainty moves EPU by more than Financial, US demand and Monetary, and Financial moves the spread by more than Uncertainty — the order-invariant encoding, preferred over a hard spread zero in a comparison set out in advance. Estimation: homoskedastic, Giannone-Lenza-Primiceri hierarchical Minnesota + sum-of-coefficients + single-unit-root priors with tightnesses chosen by marginal likelihood (the chosen θ = 1.56 is loose, and a stability check set out in advance confirmed it beats tighter settings on a standard measure of impulse-response oscillation, from Bergholt and co-authors); Covid via Cascaldi-Garcia pandemic dummies; max-root median 1.006 → rolling-window decompositions, as the near-unit-root dynamics require.

Coverage, honestly framed. Share of 2-year forecast-error variance carried by the nine named shocks, against BOTH baselines — the scheme-implied Haar-rotation prior at the same posterior parameters (the restrictions’ own contribution, which the data never test) and the mechanical 9/11 count:
TargetNamed Haar prior9/11
GDP87 [73–95]8482
CPI-U83 [64–94]8682
Unemployment Rate89 [77–96]8382
Fed Funds Rate89 [76–97]8682
For the nominal/policy block the posterior barely improves on the prior; the same two variables’ residuals are partially predictable from this production model’s information set (Forni-Gambetti p = 0.027/0.018) — the designed information gap of a system with no term-structure variable; adding the 10-year Treasury yield is a robustness check set out in advance, with a fixed rule for when the difference would matter. “Technology” is the compact-model definition (it correlates with none of this production model’s twelve shocks, max |corr| 0.15); its series and Global Demand’s are Granger-predictable from SPF forecasts (the foresight signature; Monetary passes, p = 0.199). Identification diagnostics for the adopted scheme: acceptance 9.9e-05/rotation, ESS ratio 0.48, max weight 0.5%. The scheme was frozen and adopted after an adversarial internal review set out in advance; the full trail is in docs/research/compact11_design_2026-07.md, and a one-page standalone rendering lives at compact_model.html.
Impulse responses under the adopted identification
How to read this chart
Median impulse responses with 17–83% bands to a one-standard-deviation structural shock, under the adopted identification and its volume weights — what the restrictions produce, to read against the matrix above, which is what they assume. Unrestricted cells are where the data speak; set-identification width is part of the band.
Source: FRED. Joshua Bailey Bayesian VAR · vintage 2026-07
